body.u: Here I am now at the end of my career, I collect the much coveted present for forty two whole yearsyears' service to the Fire Brigade the day after tomorrow. Following much in the path of my older brother who also was a Fireman now retired. I can still remember vividly the day it all started…

I tearedtore open the envelope anxiously, impatiently waiting to study the actual results of my exams. A big grin shot across my face as I saw English Language ‘B pass’ and then Maths ‘C pass’, my chin then hit the floor when I read ‘Physics D fail’. I knew then it would be another year at school to try to obtain the physics ‘o’ level I needed to become an apprentice as an electrical engineer. After much studying I scrapped a ‘C pass’ and then began the electrical engineers course in the September of ’79. I will never forget the day I walked out of school never to return, it was one of the sadestsaddest days of my life. I knew I had to change all of my present friends and make new ones.

With not much success at electrical engineering I left the apprenticshipapprenticeship very disappointed, as the unemployment situation was grave I was left with little hope of employment. Then on a visit to my brothers he persuaded me that I should have a ‘crack’ at the Fire Service. So with little else to do I applied with success, and realised perhaps this was what I was looking for, a job with exciting prospects.

I was twenty two years of age when I became a fully qualified Fireman, I now had a flat of my own, a small motor-cycle to get me about, plenty of girlfriends and I was really enjoying life. I was now living in the heart of London away from the increasingly boring Isle of Sheppey.

A few years later I decided that perhaps it was time I settled down, got married and live in a house. A girl I had been seeing for a long time seemed to be the right choice for me, so I married her and I sold the flat for a nice profit and moved in to a beautiful house on the out-skirts of London.

Soon after I became a proud father of twins, and so more money was needed so I studied extremely hard for promotion and with a lot of success. Everything was going perfectly when my mother and father died in the short space of two years. Life had to go on and I celebrated the beginning of a new century in real style. Life seemed to slow down by now and I was gradually watching my children grow up and eventually leave home to start off their own lives.

I was now a governor of a Fire station with nothing else to look forward to but a few more yearsyears' service, and a nice pension at the end of it all. I now have my own car, own house and a loving wife.
Well the day has come, I now get up for the last time to go to work, a very sad day but the rest will be very much appreciated.
